walkthrough.title: Distribute the Game Boards
walkthrough.text: Let's get My City set up. Each player takes one personal game board. These are double-sided boards showing a landscape grid with rocks, trees, and a river running through the middle. For your first game, use side A. If you have played through the full campaign, side B is for the Eternal Game.

walkthrough.title: Sort Your Building Tiles
walkthrough.text: Each player takes their own identical set of polyomino building tiles. These are the colorful, differently shaped pieces that you will be placing on your board during the game. Lay them all face up in front of you so you can quickly find each one when its card is flipped. Every player has the exact same set of tiles.

walkthrough.title: Prepare the Card Deck
walkthrough.text: Find the shared deck of building cards. Each card shows a picture of one specific tile shape. Shuffle the deck thoroughly and place it face down in the center of the table where everyone can reach it. This deck drives the entire game, so make sure it is well shuffled.

walkthrough.title: Understand Your Board
walkthrough.text: Take a moment to look at your board. You will see three types of important squares. Grey squares are rocks, and any rocks left uncovered at the end of the game cost you points. Green squares are trees, and trees left visible earn you points. The river divides your board into sections. Your goal is to cover rocks while preserving trees.

walkthrough.title: Check for Legacy Components
walkthrough.text: If this is your very first episode, you are all set. There are no stickers or envelope contents to worry about yet. If you are starting a new chapter, open the matching numbered envelope and read the instructions inside. It will introduce new rules, tokens, or objectives for this chapter.

walkthrough.title: Apply Any Stickers
walkthrough.text: If you have played previous episodes, make sure all stickers from past results have been applied to your board. Winners and losers receive different stickers between episodes. These permanent modifications change your scoring landscape for all future games. Verify everyone's board is up to date before starting.

walkthrough.title: Confirm the Rules
walkthrough.text: Make sure all players understand the current chapter's rules. Each chapter introduces new mechanics through its envelope contents. Review any special scoring conditions, new tile types, or objective cards that apply to this episode. Everyone should be on the same page before the first card is flipped.

walkthrough.title: Ready to Play
walkthrough.text: Setup is complete. The card deck is shuffled and central, each player has their board and full tile set arranged in front of them. Everyone plays simultaneously in My City, so there is no need to pick a first player. When everyone is ready, flip the first card and let's start building.

walkthrough.title: How a Turn Works
walkthrough.text: My City is beautifully simple. Someone flips the top card of the building deck, revealing a specific tile shape. All players simultaneously find that exact tile in their personal set. Then everyone decides where to place it on their own board. That is the entire turn. Flip, find, place. No waiting for other players.

walkthrough.title: Placement Rules
walkthrough.text: When placing a tile, there are a few rules to follow. The tile must align with the grid squares on your board. It cannot overlap any tile already placed. It cannot hang off the edge of the board. You may rotate and flip the tile freely to find the best fit.

walkthrough.title: Adjacency Requirement
walkthrough.text: In most episodes, your tiles must connect to what you have already built. Each tile you place must be adjacent to the river or to a previously placed tile. This means you build outward from the river, expanding your city step by step. You cannot place a tile in an isolated spot with no connection.

walkthrough.title: Choosing to Pass
walkthrough.text: You do not have to place every tile. If you cannot find a legal placement, or if you simply choose not to place the tile, you may pass. Set that tile aside, it will not be used this episode. There is no penalty for passing, but you miss the chance to cover rocks or build toward objectives.

walkthrough.title: Strategic Covering
walkthrough.text: Every placement decision matters because of what your tiles cover and leave exposed. Cover grey rock squares to avoid losing points at the end. Try to leave green tree squares visible to earn positive points. Sometimes a placement covers both a rock and a tree, and you need to weigh which matters more.

walkthrough.title: The River
walkthrough.text: The river is a permanent feature of your board that cannot be covered by tiles. It acts as a natural divider and your starting anchor point for tile placement. Plan your city on both sides of the river so you do not get locked out of large sections of your board.

walkthrough.title: Tile Orientation
walkthrough.text: You have complete freedom with how you orient each tile. Rotate it ninety, one hundred eighty, or two hundred seventy degrees. Flip it to its mirror image. Try different orientations before committing, because the right rotation can mean the difference between covering a rock and covering a tree.

walkthrough.title: When the Episode Ends
walkthrough.text: The episode ends when all cards in the deck have been flipped. At that point, everyone stops and scores their board. Some later chapters introduce alternative end conditions through envelope rules, but the base game always runs through the full deck.

walkthrough.title: Scoring the Episode
walkthrough.text: At the end of an episode, count up your points. Lose one point for each visible rock square that was not covered by a tile. Gain one point for each visible tree square that was not covered. Add any bonus points from completed building configurations or chapter-specific objectives. The player with the highest total wins the episode.

walkthrough.title: The Legacy Between Episodes
walkthrough.text: After scoring, the legacy system kicks in. The episode winner applies reward stickers to their board. Other players may apply catch-up stickers that provide different advantages. These stickers permanently modify your board, changing scoring zones or adding new features for all future episodes. Choose sticker placement carefully because it cannot be undone.

walkthrough.title: Chapter Progression
walkthrough.text: The campaign has eight chapters with three episodes each, for twenty-four episodes total. At the start of each new chapter, open the numbered envelope and integrate its contents. These envelopes introduce new mechanics like special tile types, new scoring conditions, and objective cards that add layers of strategy as you progress.

walkthrough.title: Tiebreaker
walkthrough.text: If two or more players tie on points, the player with fewer visible rocks wins the tie. If they are still tied after that, those players share the victory. Ties are rare because each player's board evolves differently through the legacy system.

walkthrough.title: Welcome to Your First Episode
walkthrough.text: Welcome to your first episode of My City. In this practice tutorial, we will walk through the opening turns of a game together, showing you how to make smart tile placement decisions. We will use Chapter one, Episode one rules, which means basic scoring only: negative points for visible rocks and positive points for visible trees. Let's build a city.

walkthrough.title: Your Starting Board
walkthrough.text: Look at your board. Notice where the rocks and trees are located. The rocks are the grey squares scattered around the board, and the trees are the green squares. The river cuts through the middle. Your first few tiles should go near the river, since that is your adjacency anchor point.

walkthrough.title: First Card Flip
walkthrough.text: Let's flip the first card. Everyone finds the matching tile in their set. This is a medium-sized piece with three or four squares. Look at your board near the river and find a spot where this tile covers at least one rock square. Rotate and flip the tile to test different orientations before placing it.

walkthrough.title: Covering Your First Rock
walkthrough.text: Great placement. By covering a rock on your very first tile, you have already avoided one negative point. Notice how the tile is adjacent to the river, satisfying the adjacency rule. Every tile from now on must touch the river or a previously placed tile.

walkthrough.title: Second Card Flip
walkthrough.text: The next card reveals a different tile shape. Find it in your set. This time, look for a spot that covers another rock without covering any trees. Place the tile so it connects to either the river or the tile you already placed. Remember, you can rotate and flip freely.

walkthrough.title: The Mistake: Blocking Yourself In
walkthrough.text: Here is where a common beginner mistake happens. One player places their tile tightly against the board edge, creating a narrow one-square-wide gap between the tile and the border. That gap is now almost impossible to fill because very few tiles are just one square wide. That wasted space might be hiding a rock that can never be covered.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Keep Gaps Fillable
walkthrough.text: The lesson here is to avoid creating tiny isolated gaps. When you place a tile, look at the empty spaces around it. Could another tile shape realistically fit there later? If you are leaving a one-square hole or an awkward L-shaped pocket, try a different orientation. Keep your open areas wide and connected so future tiles have room.

walkthrough.title: Third Card Flip
walkthrough.text: Another card, another tile. This one is a larger piece. A common temptation is to place it right over a cluster of trees because it fits perfectly there. Resist that urge. Trees are worth positive points. Instead, find an alternate placement that covers rocks or neutral ground, even if it means leaving a slightly messier board.

walkthrough.title: The Mistake: Covering Trees Early
walkthrough.text: One player could not resist and covered two trees with that large tile. Those are two positive points lost forever. In the base game, each visible tree is worth plus one and each visible rock is minus one. Covering a tree is effectively the same as leaving a rock uncovered. Both cost you one point.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Trees and Rocks Are Equal Opposites
walkthrough.text: Think of it this way. Covering a rock gains you one point by avoiding the penalty. Leaving a tree visible gains you one point as a bonus. Both are worth the same. So when you place a tile, try to cover rocks and avoid trees equally. Only sacrifice a tree if it lets you cover multiple rocks in exchange.

walkthrough.title: Building Across the River
walkthrough.text: By now you have a few tiles near the river on one side. Do not forget the other side. Some players build entirely on one side and then run out of good placements. Start establishing tiles on both sides of the river early so you have flexibility as different tile shapes come up.

walkthrough.title: The Mistake: Ignoring Tile Shapes Ahead
walkthrough.text: A player places a tile and creates a perfect rectangular gap, thinking any tile will fill it. But polyomino tiles come in strange shapes: T-shapes, L-shapes, S-shapes, and zigzags. That perfect rectangle might not match any tile in the set. Meanwhile, the rocks hiding underneath remain exposed.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Know Your Tile Set
walkthrough.text: Take a moment to scan your remaining tiles whenever you are deciding where to place. You know exactly which shapes are left because they are sitting right in front of you. If you leave an oddly shaped gap, check whether a remaining tile can fill it. This forward planning is the difference between experienced and beginner players.

walkthrough.title: When to Pass
walkthrough.text: A few more cards flip by and suddenly you get a tile that does not fit anywhere good. Every legal placement would cover two trees or create an unfillable gap. This is when passing is the smart move. Skip the tile, set it aside, and wait for the next card. Passing is not a failure, it is a strategic choice.

walkthrough.title: The Final Stretch
walkthrough.text: As the deck runs low, scan your board. How many rocks are still visible? How many trees have you preserved? Your last few tile placements should focus on covering any remaining rocks. At this point, efficiency matters more than elegance. A messy board that covers all its rocks beats a tidy board with exposed grey squares.

walkthrough.title: Scoring Your First Episode
walkthrough.text: The deck is empty and the episode is over. Count your visible rocks and subtract one point for each. Count your visible trees and add one point for each. The player with the highest total wins. In the campaign, the winner gets reward stickers and the other players get catch-up stickers for the next episode.

walkthrough.title: Congratulations
walkthrough.text: Congratulations, you have just played your first episode of My City. You have learned the core loop of flip, find, and place. You have seen three common mistakes: blocking yourself into unfillable gaps, covering trees carelessly, and ignoring which tile shapes remain. Remember these lessons and your city will thrive.

walkthrough.title: Before You Build Again
walkthrough.text:
Before you dive into the next episode, here are three things that can help.

First, check out General Tips for strategy basics on balancing rock coverage and tree preservation.

Second, reference the Appendix for quick definitions of all terms and mechanics.

And most importantly, ask your personal GM Guide for help anytime. Just tap the Q&A tab and ask any question during your game. Have fun building your city!

walkthrough.title: Common Mistakes to Avoid
walkthrough.text: Before we get into strategy, here are the most common mistakes new My City players make. First, creating one-square gaps that no tile can fill. Second, covering trees carelessly when rocks are available nearby. Third, building only on one side of the river and running out of space. Fourth, not scanning remaining tiles before placing, leading to wasted gaps. Keep these in mind and you will avoid the biggest point losses.

walkthrough.title: Prioritize Rock Coverage
walkthrough.text: Every visible rock at the end of the episode costs you one point. Your primary goal should be covering as many rocks as possible. When you pick up a tile, first look at where it can cover rocks. Only after you have considered all rock-covering options should you look at neutral placements. Think of rocks as debts on your board that you need to pay off with tile coverage.

walkthrough.title: Route Around Trees
walkthrough.text: Trees are your positive points, so protect them. When you have a choice between two placements and one covers a tree while the other covers empty ground, always choose the empty ground option. Sometimes you need to use a less efficient rotation to avoid a tree, but the point saved is worth the awkward placement. Only cover a tree if doing so lets you cover two or more rocks simultaneously.

walkthrough.title: Work Both Sides of the River
walkthrough.text: The river splits your board, and you need to build on both sides. A common trap is to focus heavily on one side, covering all its rocks and preserving its trees, while neglecting the other side entirely. By mid-game, you run out of good placements on your developed side and have no foothold on the other. Start placing tiles on both sides within the first few turns to maintain flexibility.

walkthrough.title: Think Two Tiles Ahead
walkthrough.text: The most impactful habit in My City is looking at your remaining tiles before placing. You know exactly which shapes are left because they are right in front of you. Before committing to a placement, glance at your unused tiles and ask yourself whether the gaps you are creating can be filled by remaining shapes. Two minutes of scanning saves five points of wasted space.

walkthrough.title: Know When to Pass
walkthrough.text: Passing is one of the most underused tools in a beginner's strategy. If the current tile would cover two trees or create an unfillable pocket, skip it. The tile is gone, but your board stays clean. Experienced players pass two or three tiles per episode and still score higher than beginners who force every tile onto their board. A clean board with a few unused tiles beats a crowded board with covered trees and wasted gaps.

walkthrough.title: Edge Placement Awareness
walkthrough.text: Board edges are dangerous. Tiles placed along the edge often create narrow, unfillable strips between themselves and the border. Before placing a tile near the edge, check whether the gap it creates against the board boundary matches a remaining tile shape. If not, scoot the tile inward by one square. A little planning near edges prevents the most common source of wasted space.

walkthrough.title: Adapt to the Legacy
walkthrough.text: As the campaign progresses, stickers change your board and new rules change your priorities. What worked in Chapter one might not work in Chapter four. At the start of each new chapter, re-evaluate your board. Look at where your stickers are, what new scoring conditions apply, and adjust your placement strategy accordingly. The best players treat each chapter as a fresh puzzle with familiar mechanics.

Don't build on only one side of the river
Always scan remaining tiles before placing

walkthrough.title: Zone Planning
walkthrough.text: Before you place your first tile, mentally divide your board into zones. Identify clusters of rocks that need covering, clusters of trees that need protecting, and neutral areas that serve as connective tissue. Plan your tile placement to flow through rock zones first, route around tree zones, and use neutral areas as buffers. This pre-planning prevents the haphazard placement that costs beginners points.

walkthrough.title: Tile Shape Probability
walkthrough.text: You know every tile in your set and every card in the deck. Use that information. If you have already placed your large straight pieces, the remaining tiles are mostly compact or angular shapes. Plan your gaps accordingly. Leave angular gaps for angular tiles and open areas for larger pieces. The deck does not care about your board state, so you must plan around what remains in your tile set.

walkthrough.title: River-Side Balance
walkthrough.text: Advanced players count the rocks and trees on each side of the river before the episode begins. If one side has more rocks, that side gets priority for early tile placements. If one side has more trees, plan to route tiles carefully there to avoid covering them. This asymmetric approach often outperforms the instinct to build evenly on both sides.

walkthrough.title: Defensive Passing
walkthrough.text: Passing is not just about avoiding bad placements. Advanced players sometimes pass a tile they could place decently because they know a harder-to-place tile is still in their set. By keeping board space open for that awkward shape, they avoid creating a situation where they are forced to skip the difficult tile later with rocks still exposed underneath. Think of passes as investments in future flexibility.

walkthrough.title: Sticker Placement Strategy
walkthrough.text: Sticker placement is the most consequential decision in the campaign because it is permanent. When choosing where to put a sticker, think three episodes ahead. Place scoring stickers where they will consistently generate points across multiple episodes. Place terrain stickers to neutralize the most problematic areas of your board. Avoid placing stickers in areas that are already well-managed.

walkthrough.title: The Catch-Up Mechanic
walkthrough.text: Losing an episode is not always bad. The catch-up stickers given to non-winners can be strategically powerful, sometimes more so than the winner's reward. If you are behind in the campaign standings, focus on maximizing the value of your catch-up stickers rather than fighting for a marginal win. A well-placed catch-up sticker can set you up for several future episode victories.

walkthrough.title: Adapting to New Chapter Rules
walkthrough.text: Each chapter envelope changes the game. When new scoring conditions appear, completely re-evaluate your board. Some chapters make certain areas of your board more valuable than before. Others introduce tokens or objectives that shift your placement priorities entirely. The worst mistake an advanced player can make is playing Chapter four with a Chapter one strategy.

walkthrough.title: Eternal Game Mastery
walkthrough.text: After completing the campaign, the Eternal Game uses the B-side of your board with all mechanics unlocked. Without the legacy progression, you need to score well in a single standalone game. Focus on maximizing the scoring conditions printed on your board side B. Study the full tile set and practice placing the most awkward shapes first, leaving easy-to-place tiles for the end game when board space is tight.

bullets: Mentally divide board into rock zones, tree zones, neutral zones

Plan gaps around what's left, not what's gone

entries.definition: A building piece made up of connected squares, similar to Tetris pieces. Tiles come in various shapes and sizes. Each tile matches one card in the shared deck.
A double-sided board showing a landscape grid. Side A is used during the campaign with sticker modifications. Side B is used for the Eternal Game after the campaign is complete.
The shared deck of cards that drives the game. Each card shows a specific tile shape. When a card is flipped, all players simultaneously find and place the matching tile from their set.

Permanent adhesive modifications applied to game boards between episodes. Winners and losers receive different stickers that change scoring zones, add features, or alter terrain for all future episodes.
Eight numbered envelopes containing new rules, components, and objectives. One envelope is opened at the start of each new chapter, progressively adding complexity to the game.
A grey square on the game board. Each rock left uncovered at the end of an episode costs one negative point. Covering rocks with tiles is a primary scoring objective.
A green square on the game board. Each tree left visible at the end of an episode earns one positive point. Preserving trees by routing tiles around them is a key strategy.
A permanent feature running through each game board that cannot be covered by tiles. The river divides the board into sections and serves as the starting anchor point for the adjacency rule.
Open terrain squares on the board with no special properties. Covering light squares neither costs nor earns points. They serve as neutral ground for tile placement.
Tiles must be placed adjacent to the river or to a previously placed tile. This means you build outward from the river, expanding your city step by step. No isolated placements are allowed.
All tiles must align precisely with the square grid on your board. Tiles cannot be placed at angles or between grid lines.
Tiles cannot be placed on top of other tiles already on the board. Each grid square can hold at most one tile.
Every square of a tile must fit within the board boundaries. Tiles cannot extend beyond the edge of the board.
Tiles may be freely rotated in ninety-degree increments and flipped to their mirror image. This gives maximum flexibility for finding the optimal placement on your board.
Choosing not to place a tile when its card is flipped. The tile is set aside and not used for the remainder of the episode. Passing has no direct penalty but means missed coverage opportunities.
A single game session within the campaign. Each episode plays through the entire building card deck. The campaign contains twenty-four episodes organized into eight chapters of three episodes each.

A group of three episodes in the campaign. At the start of each new chapter, players open a sealed envelope containing new rules, components, and objectives that apply to all three episodes in that chapter.
The game mechanic where permanent changes are made to game boards between episodes. Stickers, new rules, and modified components accumulate over the campaign, making each player's board unique.
Stickers given to players who did not win the episode. These often provide powerful advantages to keep the campaign competitive. Sometimes losing an episode yields better stickers than winning.
Stickers given to the episode winner. These modify the winning player's board with scoring bonuses or terrain improvements for future episodes.

A standalone, replayable game mode available after completing the twenty-four episode campaign. Uses the B-side of each game board with all mechanics from the campaign. No further permanent modifications are made.
All players place tiles at the same time after each card flip. There is no turn order, which eliminates downtime and keeps the game moving quickly.

An empty space on your board that cannot be covered because no remaining tile shape fits it. Common causes include one-square-wide corridors, board-edge strips, and oddly shaped pockets. A key strategic concern.
An advanced technique of mentally dividing your board into rock zones, tree zones, and neutral areas before placing tiles. Helps prioritize which areas to cover and which to protect.
